lenses.guidance: The chapter builds a calendar of seven appointed times: Sabbath (v 3), Passover (vv 4-8), Firstfruits (vv 9-14), Weeks (vv 15-22), Trumpets (vv 23-25), Atonement (vv 26-32), Tabernacles (vv 33-43). The form is a liturgical year.

vv 4-8's Passover — slain, blood-marked, eaten in haste — is what 1 Cor 5:7 names: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' The first feast in Lev 23's cycle anchors the gospel's central vocabulary.

vv 9-14's Firstfruits — wave the first sheaf of the harvest — is the type 1 Cor 15:20-23 names: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' The pattern is resurrection.

vv 15-22's count of fifty days from Firstfruits sets the calendar Acts 2 enacts — the Spirit's outpouring lands on the day Israel celebrated covenant grain. The redemptive arc unfolds on Lev 23's clock.

vv 33-43's Tabernacles — booths, branches, the dwelling-with-the-people image — echoes throughout Scripture into Jn 7:37-39's living water and Rev 21:3's 'God's dwelling place is now among the people.'

vv 39-43's command to live in booths recalls the LORD's wilderness provision. To worship today is to remember being delivered — the feast trains the heart to repent of self-sufficiency and trust the One who tabernacled among his people.
